look.prediction: Creamy spicy and it looks like it would taste like burnt sugar but I know better. also you see the boba pearls at the bottom peaking out from the orange liquid and its not clear from the visual what it will taste like but you know it will be more solid than the liquid tea part.

smell.association: a specific, warm incense from an Indian bookstore like a distant, dry, aromatic spice that feels locked behind the immediate sweetness.

firstBite.notes: The weird part of this drink is the sensation of the boba traveling up the straw because it feels "wrong" but it tastes good so you go with it anyway and are hit with a sudden collision of temperatures and textures. the liquid is bright and creamy and very sweet with the tea flavor i guess tannin flavor maybe its bitter? but that plus the chewy boba is kind of molasses-heavy.

arc.aftertaste: the black tea flavor lingers warm in the throat and the immediate sensation on the tongue is the tacky sugar film coating it.
arc.wildcard: The psychological shift from sipping smooth sweet and spicy liquid to having to chew something slightly warmer doesn't sound like it would work but it does (for me).

look.prediction: a heavy front-loaded heat that hits the tongue immediately from the broth and the scallions will have a soft, wilted crunch. the charred porkbelly on top should be savory and melting fat under the dry seaweed strip with add a sharp and salty ocean hit to the soup.

smell.association: it smells like nostalgia, a high-end version of classic cup ramen and thats probably because those were my first intros to ramen in general so they're kind of a baseline.

firstBite.notes: Three separate bites, first the egg then porkbelly then sip of soup and the spice intensity grows from egg to pork to soup. Egg is rubbery and unexpectedly pickled flavor with a slight vinegar tinge but also has a powdery york that dissolves as you chew. The porkbelly is tender and beautifully greasy and savory through the seaweed saltiness which takes a backseat to the pork. The broth is the real star since the spice hits immediately and strong and you feel the tingle in your tongue and lips like a lightning bolt and tastes kind of like a spicy gold coin with depth and zero grease.

arc.aftertaste: there's an an intense, stinging heat that stays on lips and both sides of tongue. The onion gets more noticeable and becomes much louder than it was mid-bite, while a deeper, more hollow umami replaces the heavy broth. under it is the ghost of the noodles leaves a surprisingly smooth, butteriness.
arc.wildcard: its a carnival of textures and flavors. you go from sharp scallions to springy noodles, down to the sudden snap of buried bean sprouts, plus a chewy little fish cake circle thing with the pink swirl. add in the second egg half and pork slice too, every bite is a different burst of color. the savory-spice broth is like the night sky and the ingredients are like different fireworks exploding against it.

look.prediction: it looks like it will taste crispy on the outside and soft on the inside and i think the sauce will be tangy and the flakes will be smoky

smell.association: surprisingly sharp almost chemical smell right out the gate. very salty and marine with a strange edge that kind of smells like hot plastic or silicon. i took one of the balls out of the container to make sure the plastic smell was actually on the food and its like industrial-oceanic sharpness all around it.

firstBite.notes: immediately hit with hyper-dense competing profiles that feels like a sensory overload. mouthfeel is soft and squish heavy which somehow amplifies the chaotic rush of the batter flavor with heavy fat and sharp acidity hitting the palate, not unfolding in layers.

arc.aftertaste: quickly fades into a simple salty ocean with light starchy coating.
arc.wildcard: looks great but bombs execution imho since i do like takoyaki elsewhere but these guys really amped up the marine funk flavor to the point it's on the edge of being rancid. it could be more crunchy outside maybe if eaten right away when hot but maybe not because on close inspection of the bite you can see the batter wasn't super crispy to begin with so it was probably squishy the whole time. all that said still interesting experience glad we got it and i ate half of the container anyway but i wouldn't order this particular thing from these guys in the future unless you want that ocean funk, then they nail it.

look.prediction: so the look is hard to describe and it doesn't fit into existing categories above so its like a pale yellow color that looks soft and moist but not so much wet/glistening i guess to me. and i think they'll be chewy and a little salty.

smell.association: it reminds me of a mall food court and also smells like a clean sole of new sneakers. a warm starchy oil aroma that triggers food nostalgia and fresh retail at the same time.

firstBite.notes: satisfying chewy texture, expected, with a filling that has a heavy and savory pork punch packed with loud onion and maybe garlic or something that tastes spicy without the heat.

arc.aftertaste: light and clean savory aftertaste with strong onion lingering with the salt.
arc.wildcard: this is consistently good from first bite to last. there are a few tiny subtle crunchy bits hidden inside the filling texture but too tiny to change the overall sensation of soft and chewy.

look.prediction: ok the donuts themselves are golden and matte and crispy-looking while the sauce is white/cream and glossy and wet/glistening. looks like these will have a good crunch-to-chew ratio. tehy should have a deep caramelized starchy sweetness from the batter with an oil-and-dough interior thats begging to be dipped in the sweet cum sauce lol

smell.association: smells like walking into a classic bakery in the morning mixed with a specific memory of baking bread with sarah that one summer. warm yeasty smell with sweetess on top

firstBite.notes: without the sauce its a classic thai donut, so deep charred sugar crunch on the outside yielding to a soft, pillowy, slightly buttery expanse of warm bread inside. with a distinct sort of burnt caramelized edge to the outside crisp, in a good way. with the dipping sauce it gets rid of the charred flavor and replaces it with a creamy sweet milk layer making the warm and yeasty dough flavor shine.

arc.aftertaste: thick film of sugar on tongue from the sauce and as it fades theres a buttery richness that is stronger than when eating it.
arc.wildcard: standard execution, good stuff. high-heat fry does leave the outer shell a bit dry so need to drink between bites. the contrast from exterior to interior is the fun part of this and the sauce makes it, not as good without it.
